---
title: "Ch 8a — Introduction to Trigonometry"
subtitle: "Three ratios — sine, cosine, tangent — that depend on the angle, not on the size"
lesson-id: "cbse10.trigonometry-intro"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 8
ncert-chapter-title: "Introduction to Trigonometry"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse10.triangles-similarity, t2.pythagorean-theorem]
unlocks: [cbse10.trigonometric-identities, cbse10.heights-and-distances, cbse11.trigonometric-functions]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state when two right triangles are *similar*? See
   [`cbse10.triangles-similarity`](../06-triangles-similarity/index.qmd).
2. Can you state and use Pythagoras: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$?
3. Can you simplify $\sqrt{3}/2$ — that is, leave it in surd form, not
   convert to decimal?
4. Do you know what an angle in degrees is — e.g. $30°, 45°, 60°$?

Three yeses, ready.
:::

## Touch

A right triangle has one right angle, plus two acute angles. Fix one of
the acute angles — call it $\theta$ — and ask: as the triangle gets
*bigger* but keeps the same angle $\theta$, what stays the same?

```{ojs}
viewof theta = Inputs.range([15, 75], {value: 30, step: 5, label: "Angle θ (degrees)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof size = Inputs.range([1, 4], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "Triangle size"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 280, padX = 20, padY = 20;
  const rad = theta * Math.PI / 180;
  const adj = 100 * size, opp = adj * Math.tan(rad), hyp = adj / Math.cos(rad);
  const A = [padX, h - padY], B = [padX + adj, h - padY], C = [padX + adj, h - padY - opp];
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  // triangle
  svg.append("polygon").attr("points", `${A.join(",")} ${B.join(",")} ${C.join(",")}`)
    .attr("fill", "rgba(38,102,170,0.1)").attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // labels
  svg.append("text").attr("x", A[0] + 30).attr("y", A[1] - 4).attr("font-size", 12).text("θ");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", (A[0] + B[0]) / 2).attr("y", A[1] + 15).attr("font-size", 11)
    .text(`adjacent ${(adj / 100).toFixed(2)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", B[0] + 4).attr("y", (A[1] + C[1]) / 2).attr("font-size", 11)
    .text(`opposite ${(opp / 100).toFixed(2)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", (A[0] + C[0]) / 2 - 50).attr("y", (A[1] + C[1]) / 2 - 6)
    .attr("font-size", 11).text(`hypotenuse ${(hyp / 100).toFixed(2)}`);
  // ratios panel
  const sinT = opp / hyp, cosT = adj / hyp, tanT = opp / adj;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 14).attr("font-size", 13)
    .text(`opp/hyp = ${sinT.toFixed(3)},  adj/hyp = ${cosT.toFixed(3)},  opp/adj = ${tanT.toFixed(3)}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

Drag the **size** slider while keeping $\theta$ fixed. Watch the three
ratios at the bottom. They do not change. Now change $\theta$ — the ratios
change immediately.

That is the central observation: the ratios depend on the **angle**, not
on the **size**.

## Wonder

1. The widget reports three ratios. Why do they not change when you change
   the size?
2. If $\theta = 0°$, what would the triangle look like? What about
   $\theta = 90°$?
3. The widget had three "labelled" sides: opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse.
   Which one is *always* the longest? Why?
4. Suppose someone tells you only the value of one ratio (e.g. opp/hyp =
   $0.6$). Could you reconstruct the angle?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Similar right triangles have proportional sides — that's *Class 10
Ch. 6 BPT* in disguise. (3) The hypotenuse is opposite the right angle,
and the right angle is the largest angle, so the hypotenuse is the
longest side. (4) Yes — that's what the inverse-trig functions do.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Three ratios for a 3-4-5 triangle
A right triangle has legs $3$ and $4$, hypotenuse $5$. The angle $\theta$
faces the side of length $3$. Compute opp/hyp, adj/hyp, opp/adj.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Opposite to $\theta$ is the side facing $\theta$, which is $3$. Adjacent is
$4$. Hyp is $5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp/hyp = $3/5$. adj/hyp = $4/5$. opp/adj = $3/4$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Why don't they change?
Same right triangle, but now scaled up by a factor of $5$ (so legs $15, 20$,
hypotenuse $25$). The angle is still $\theta$. Recompute the three ratios.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp/hyp = $15/25 = 3/5$. adj/hyp = $20/25 = 4/5$. opp/adj = $15/20 = 3/4$.
**Identical.** Reason: the new triangle is *similar* to the old one
(same shape, scaled), so corresponding sides are in the same ratio.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Specific angles
Build (or imagine) a $30°{-}60°{-}90°$ triangle with hypotenuse $2$. The side
opposite $30°$ has length $1$ (a known fact from Class 9 geometry, derived
from an equilateral split). The side opposite $60°$ has length $\sqrt{3}$.
Compute the three ratios for $\theta = 30°$ and for $\theta = 60°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For $\theta = 30°$: opposite is $1$, adjacent is $\sqrt{3}$, hyp is $2$.
For $\theta = 60°$: opposite is $\sqrt{3}$, adjacent is $1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\theta = 30°$: opp/hyp = $1/2$, adj/hyp = $\sqrt{3}/2$, opp/adj = $1/\sqrt{3}$.

$\theta = 60°$: opp/hyp = $\sqrt{3}/2$, adj/hyp = $1/2$, opp/adj = $\sqrt{3}$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — A 45° triangle
A right isoceles triangle has both legs of length $1$, hypotenuse
$\sqrt{2}$. With $\theta = 45°$, find the three ratios.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp/hyp = $1/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}/2$. adj/hyp = $1/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}/2$.
opp/adj = $1/1 = 1$.

(All three are clean numbers — that is why $30°, 45°, 60°$ are the
**special angles**.)
</details>

### Problem 5 — Sine and cosine of complementary angles
For the $30°-60°-90°$ triangle in Problem 3, you found that
opp/hyp at $30°$ equals adj/hyp at $60°$. Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the two acute angles in *any* right triangle. What do they sum to?
What is the *opposite* of one angle compared to the *adjacent* of the
other?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$30 + 60 = 90$, so they are **complementary**. The side opposite to one
acute angle is adjacent to the other. So the ratio "opp/hyp" computed at
one acute angle equals "adj/hyp" at the complementary angle. This will
become the rule $\sin(90° - \theta) = \cos\theta$ in *Name*.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Inverse: from ratio to angle
A right triangle has opp/hyp = $1/2$. What is $\theta$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You computed this in Problem 3.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\theta = 30°$.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

In a right triangle, with $\theta$ one of the acute angles, define:

$$
\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \qquad
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \qquad
\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}.
$$

The reciprocals are
$$\csc\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}, \qquad \sec\theta = \dfrac{1}
{\cos\theta}, \qquad \cot\theta = \dfrac{1}{\tan\theta}.$$

(Note: in CBSE notation, $\csc$ is written $\text{cosec}$.)

**Special angles** — memorise this table:

| $\theta$ | $0°$ | $30°$ | $45°$ | $60°$ | $90°$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\sin\theta$ | $0$ | $1/2$ | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | $1$ |
| $\cos\theta$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ |
| $\tan\theta$ | $0$ | $1/\sqrt{3}$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{3}$ | undef. |

**Complementary-angle rule** (for $0° < \theta < 90°$):
$$\sin(90° - \theta) = \cos\theta, \quad \cos(90° - \theta) = \sin\theta,
\quad \tan(90° - \theta) = \cot\theta,$$
and similarly for the reciprocals.

:::

A verse:

> *Sine is opposite over hypotenuse high.*
> *Cosine, adjacent over hypotenuse, never to lie.*
> *Tangent? Opposite over adjacent — that's the way.*
> *Same shape, same ratios — for any size, any day.*

(Memorisation hint: **SOH-CAH-TOA** — Sin = Opp/Hyp, Cos = Adj/Hyp,
Tan = Opp/Adj.)

## Apply

### Worked example

> In a right triangle, $\sin A = \dfrac{3}{5}$. Find $\cos A$ and $\tan A$.

The triangle has opposite $= 3$ and hypotenuse $= 5$ (or any scalar
multiple). By Pythagoras, adjacent $= \sqrt{25 - 9} = 4$.

So $\cos A = \dfrac{4}{5}$ and $\tan A = \dfrac{3}{4}$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\sin\theta = \dfrac{12}{13}$. Find $\cos\theta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp = 12, hyp = 13. adj = $\sqrt{169 - 144} = 5$. $\cos\theta = 5/13$.
</details>

**W2.** Compute $\sin 30° + \cos 60°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1/2 + 1/2 = 1$.
</details>

**W3.** Compute $\tan 45° \cdot \cos 60° + \sin 30°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 \cdot 1/2 + 1/2 = 1$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** If $\tan\theta = \dfrac{4}{3}$, find $\sin\theta + \cos\theta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set up a 3-4-5 right triangle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp = 4, adj = 3, hyp = 5. $\sin\theta = 4/5, \cos\theta = 3/5$. Sum =
$7/5$.
</details>

**P2.** Evaluate $\dfrac{\sin 30° + \tan 45° - \csc 60°}{\sec 30° + \cos 60°
+ \cot 45°}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plug in special-angle values.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Numerator: $\frac{1}{2} + 1 - \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3}{2} - \frac{2}
{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} - 4}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
Denominator: $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} + \frac{1}{2} + 1 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} +
\frac{3}{2} = \frac{4 + 3\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
Ratio = $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3} - 4}{3\sqrt{3} + 4}$. Rationalise:
$\dfrac{(3\sqrt{3} - 4)^2}{27 - 16} = \dfrac{43 - 24\sqrt{3}}{11}$.
</details>

**P3.** $\sin(A + B) = 1$ and $\sin(A - B) = \dfrac{1}{2}$ with $A, B$
acute. Find $A$ and $B$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin x = 1$ when $x = 90°$. $\sin x = 1/2$ when $x = 30°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A + B = 90°$, $A - B = 30°$. Solve: $A = 60°, B = 30°$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** In a right triangle $ABC$, right-angled at $B$, $AB = 24, BC = 7$.
Find $\sin A, \cos A, \tan C$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Hypotenuse first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AC = \sqrt{576 + 49} = 25$. From $A$'s viewpoint: opposite is $BC = 7$,
adjacent is $AB = 24$. $\sin A = 7/25$, $\cos A = 24/25$.
From $C$'s viewpoint: opposite is $AB = 24$, adjacent is $BC = 7$. $\tan C
= 24/7$.
</details>

**S2.** Express $\sin 67° + \cos 75°$ in terms of trigonometric ratios of
acute angles between $0°$ and $45°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin(90° - x) = \cos x$, etc.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 67° = \sin(90° - 23°) = \cos 23°$. $\cos 75° = \cos(90° - 15°) =
\sin 15°$. So **$\sin 67° + \cos 75° = \cos 23° + \sin 15°$**.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** $\sin 60° \cdot \cos 30° = $
(a) $\dfrac{1}{4}$  (b) $\dfrac{1}{2}$  (c) $\dfrac{3}{4}$  (d) $1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{3}{4}$. **(c).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — MCQ).** If $\sin\theta = \cos\theta$ and $\theta$ is acute,
then $\theta = $
(a) $30°$ (b) $45°$ (c) $60°$ (d) $90°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 45°$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** If $\sec\theta = \dfrac{13}{12}$, find $\sin\theta$ and
$\tan\theta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\cos\theta = 12/13$, so adj = 12, hyp = 13.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
opp = $\sqrt{169 - 144} = 5$. $\sin\theta = 5/13, \tan\theta = 5/12$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** Evaluate $\dfrac{\sin 30°}{\cos 45°} + \dfrac{\cos 60°}
{\sin 45°} - \dfrac{\sin 60°}{\tan 45°}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1/2}{\sqrt{2}/2} + \dfrac{1/2}{\sqrt{2}/2} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}/2}{1}
= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
= \sqrt{2} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{3}}{2}$.
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** In $\triangle ABC$, right-angled at $B$, $AB = 5$,
$\angle ACB = 30°$. Find the lengths of sides $BC$ and $AC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At angle $C = 30°$: opposite is $AB = 5$, adjacent is $BC$, hyp is $AC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan 30° = \dfrac{AB}{BC} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{5}{BC}
\Rightarrow BC = 5\sqrt{3}$.
$\sin 30° = \dfrac{AB}{AC} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{5}{AC}
\Rightarrow AC = 10$.
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** Without using trig tables, evaluate
$$\dfrac{\sec 41° \sin 49° + \cos 49° \csc 41°}{\sin^2 38° + \sin^2 52°}.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$41 + 49 = 90$ and $38 + 52 = 90$. Use complementary identities.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Numerator: $\sec 41° = \csc 49°$, so $\sec 41° \cdot \sin 49° = \csc 49°
\cdot \sin 49° = 1$. Similarly, $\cos 49° \csc 41° = \sin 41° \sec 41° = $
hmm, actually $\csc 41° = \sec 49°$, so $\cos 49° \csc 41° = \cos 49° \sec
49° = 1$. Numerator = $2$.
Denominator: $\sin 52° = \cos 38°$, so $\sin^2 38° + \cos^2 38° = 1$.
Ratio = $\boxed{2}$.
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** (a) Show that $(1 + \tan A + \sec A)(1 +
\cot A - \csc A) = 2$. (b) Hence evaluate the expression at $A = 45°$ as a
sanity check.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Convert everything to $\sin$ and $\cos$. Common-denominate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Numerator after common-denominator manipulation: $(\cos A + \sin A + 1)
(\sin A + \cos A - 1)/(\sin A \cos A) = $ difference of squares.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a)** $(1 + \tan A + \sec A)(1 + \cot A - \csc A)$
$= \left(1 + \dfrac{\sin A}{\cos A} + \dfrac{1}{\cos A}\right)
\left(1 + \dfrac{\cos A}{\sin A} - \dfrac{1}{\sin A}\right)$
$= \dfrac{\cos A + \sin A + 1}{\cos A} \cdot \dfrac{\sin A + \cos A - 1}{\sin A}$
$= \dfrac{(\sin A + \cos A)^2 - 1^2}{\sin A \cos A}$
$= \dfrac{\sin^2 A + 2 \sin A \cos A + \cos^2 A - 1}{\sin A \cos A}$
$= \dfrac{1 + 2 \sin A \cos A - 1}{\sin A \cos A}$
$= \dfrac{2 \sin A \cos A}{\sin A \cos A} = \mathbf{2}$. $\square$

**(b)** At $A = 45°$: $\tan 45° = 1, \sec 45° = \sqrt{2}, \cot 45° = 1,
\csc 45° = \sqrt{2}$. Expression = $(1 + 1 + \sqrt{2})(1 + 1 - \sqrt{2}) =
(2 + \sqrt{2})(2 - \sqrt{2}) = 4 - 2 = 2$. ✓
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 8.3 Q1–Q12 cover special-angle
evaluations like Q4; Exemplar 8.4 Q1–Q12 are full identity simplifications
like Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q22 mirrors Q5. CBSE 2023
Standard, Q35 mirrors Q7.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\sin 30° = 0.5$, so $\sin 60° = $ double, $= 1$."** Wrong. Sine is
   *not* linear. $\sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$. The special-angle
   table is not a multiplicative pattern.
2. **"$\tan 90°$ is some big number."** $\tan 90°$ is **undefined**
   (denominator zero). Do not write a number for it on the board script.
3. **"$\sin\theta$ can be more than 1."** Never, in a right triangle:
   opp $\le$ hyp, so $\sin\theta \in [0, 1]$. Same for $\cos$.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive the special-angle table from the $30°{-}60°{-}90°$ and
   $45°{-}45°{-}90°$ geometries, without notes?
2. Did Q6 and Q7 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Could you spot, in an unlabelled right triangle, which side is "opposite
   to $\theta$" and which is "adjacent"?

- **3 yes:** move on to identities.
- **2 yes:** redo P2 and Q7. Trig algebra rewards drill.
- **0–1 yes:** the special-angle table is the foundation of *Identities*
  and *Heights & Distances*. Do not move on until it is automatic.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse10.triangles-similarity`](../06-triangles-similarity/index.qmd) —
  similar right triangles have proportional sides; that's why ratios depend
  only on the angle.
- [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd) —
  closing the loop between two known sides and the third.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.trigonometric-identities`](../08b-trigonometric-identities/index.qmd) —
  the algebraic rules that follow from these ratios.
- [`cbse10.heights-and-distances`](../09-heights-and-distances/index.qmd) —
  applications.
- [`cbse11.trigonometric-functions`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/03a-trig-functions/index.qmd) —
  the unit-circle generalisation.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 8.1–8.3: trigonometric ratios,
  ratios of complementary angles. (Section 8.4 — trigonometric identities
  — is treated in **Lesson 9 (Ch 8b)** of this tier.)
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  - [`t3.trig-ratios`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/07-trig-ratios/index.qmd) —
    the ratios from a synthesis angle.
  - [`t3.unit-circle`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd) —
    extending beyond acute angles.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every "rotate the spaceship by $\theta$" call in a
2D engine evaluates $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ to map old coordinates
to new. Same for character pathfinding around obstacles.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Sine and cosine are the building blocks of *Fourier
analysis* — the decomposition of any periodic signal into pure tones.
Audio compression (MP3), image compression (JPEG), and signal
processing all rest on it.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Better Explained (the "ratios depend on angle, not
  size" framing); Russian school (problem-first); Bhāskara (compact
  *Name* table + verse).
- **Source notes:** Q7 is the NCERT-canonical "show this identity equals
  2" pattern; Q6 is the canonical complementary-angle simplification.
:::
